name: Gamma-Glutamyl Transferase (GGT)
summary: GGT is an enzyme concentrated in the liver and bile ducts (also present in kidney, pancreas, and other tissues). It is a sensitive marker of bile-duct (cholestatic) stress and is frequently elevated by alcohol and enzyme-inducing drugs.
whatItMeans: An elevated GGT, especially together with a raised alkaline phosphatase (ALP), supports a cholestatic (bile-flow obstruction or bile-duct) pattern of liver injury. GGT is also used to confirm that an isolated ALP elevation is of liver rather than bone origin, since GGT is not raised by bone disease. Isolated GGT elevation is common with alcohol use and certain medications and is not specific by itself. This is educational information, not a diagnosis.
raises: Fatty liver disease (MASLD/NAFLD) and metabolic syndrome
lowers: Resolution of the underlying liver or biliary condition
confounders: Highly sensitive but not specific; elevation can occur with many drugs and non-liver conditions
Alcohol intake in the days before testing can raise GGT
Not elevated by bone disease, so it helps localize an ALP rise to the liver
Reference upper limit differs by sex and lab

ranges.note: General adult reference range; varies by lab and sex.
